content.text: What an Update Domain Is

An update domain (UD) is a logical group of VMs in an Availability Set that Azure resets together during planned maintenance. When the Azure platform needs to apply host-level patches — firmware updates, hypervisor patches, hardware microcode fixes — it must reboot the underlying host servers. Azure does this one update domain at a time, ensuring that the VMs in other update domains remain running while one domain undergoes maintenance.

The key guarantee: Azure will never reboot two update domains simultaneously from the same Availability Set. Between each UD reboot, Azure waits for the VMs in the just-rebooted UD to return to a healthy state before moving to the next UD. This sequential rollout means your application keeps serving traffic throughout a maintenance cycle, at reduced but not zero capacity.

attrs.alt: Update Domain sequential maintenance: UD0 reboots first, recovers, then UD1 reboots, then UD2 — only one UD offline at any time

content.text: How Update Domains Work

When you create an Availability Set with N update domains (default 5, max 20), Azure assigns each VM to a UD numbered 0 through N-1. The assignment is deterministic: the first VM goes to UD0, the second to UD1, and so on, cycling through the UDs round-robin as you add VMs.
Sequential maintenance:
Azure processes UDs in order: UD0, then UD1, then UD2, etc. After each UD, it checks that the just-updated VMs passed health checks before proceeding.
Health verification gate:
Azure monitors VM heartbeats and load balancer health probe results. If a UD's VMs don't come back healthy after a configured timeout, Azure may pause or abort the maintenance cycle.
Advance notice:
planned maintenance events that require reboots appear in Azure Service Health 35 days before they begin. This gives you time to schedule workload-specific actions.
Not all maintenance requires reboots:

most platform updates are memory-preserving (live updates to the hypervisor) and do not trigger a UD rotation. VMs pause for under 30 seconds without a reboot. UD logic applies only to reboot-required events.
Creating an Availability Set with Custom Update Domains

# Create an AS with 5 update domains (default) and 3 fault domains
az vm availability-set create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAS \
  --location eastus \
  --platform-fault-domain-count 3 \
  --platform-update-domain-count 5

# Create an AS with 10 update domains for finer-grained rolling updates
az vm availability-set create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myFineGrainedAS \
  --location eastus \
  --platform-fault-domain-count 3 \
  --platform-update-domain-count 10

# View the UD assignment for a specific VM
az vm get-instance-view \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-1 \
  --query "instanceView.platformUpdateDomain"

# List all VMs with their UD assignments
az vm list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --show-details \
  --query "[?availabilitySet!=null].{name:name, ud:instanceView.platformUpdateDomain, fd:instanceView.platformFaultDomain}" \
  --output table
Update Domains vs Fault Domains: The Two Axes of Protection

Availability Sets use fault domains and update domains as orthogonal axes. A VM is assigned to exactly one fault domain AND exactly one update domain. The goal of the placement algorithm is to ensure that no two VMs from the same Availability Set share both the same FD and the same UD. This gives two-dimensional protection:
Protected Against
Mechanism
Result
Unplanned hardware failure (PDU, ToR switch)
Fault Domains
VMs in different racks; single rack failure takes only 1/N VMs offline
Planned platform maintenance (host patches, firmware)
Update Domains
Sequential reboot; only 1/N of VMs offline at any time during maintenance
Both simultaneously (unlikely but possible)
FD + UD combination
VMs in same UD are in different FDs, so a FD failure during maintenance reduces but does not eliminate capacity

An ideal 9-VM AS with 3 FDs and 3 UDs has each combination of FD and UD occupied by exactly one VM (a 3x3 matrix). This is the most spread-out configuration, minimizing correlated impact from both failure types simultaneously.
Monitoring and Responding to Planned Maintenance

Azure announces planned maintenance events that require host reboots through Service Health at least 35 days in advance. During the maintenance window, you can use the following approaches to minimize impact:
Check Scheduled Maintenance Status

# Check if a VM has pending maintenance
az vm get-instance-view \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-1 \
  --query "instanceView.maintenanceRedeployStatus"

# Check all VMs in a resource group for pending maintenance
az vm list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --show-details \
  --query "[?instanceView.maintenanceRedeployStatus.isCustomerInitiatedMaintenanceAllowed==true].{name:name, maintenanceWindow:instanceView.maintenanceRedeployStatus.maintenanceWindowEndTime}" \
  --output table

# Subscribe to Service Health planned maintenance alerts
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name "PlannedMaintenanceAlert" \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)" \
  --condition "category=PlannedMaintenance" \
  --action-groups /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/myAG
Pre-Drain VMs Before Maintenance Window

If you have a load balancer, you can remove a VM from the backend pool before its maintenance window to pre-drain connections, allowing in-flight requests to complete before the host reboots. This eliminates the abrupt connection termination that occurs when the host reboots with active connections.

# Remove a VM's NIC from the LB backend pool before maintenance
az network nic ip-config address-pool remove \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --nic-name myVM-1-NIC \
  --ip-config-name ipconfig1 \
  --lb-name myLB \
  --address-pool myBackendPool

# Optionally: redeploy the VM now (moving it to a fresh host) to avoid the future maintenance window
az vm redeploy \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-1

# Re-add to the backend pool after reboot completes
az network nic ip-config address-pool add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --nic-name myVM-1-NIC \
  --ip-config-name ipconfig1 \
  --lb-name myLB \
  --address-pool myBackendPool
Update Domains and VMSS Rolling Upgrades
Virtual Machine Scale Sets (VMSS) have their own rolling upgrade policy that mirrors update domain behavior but for scale set instances. VMSS supports three upgrade modes:
Upgrade Mode
Behavior
Best For
Manual
You trigger upgrades explicitly per batch
Full control; production workloads with custom release processes
Automatic
Platform upgrades instances as soon as a new model is available; no batching guarantee
Dev/test; stateless workloads where brief downtime per instance is acceptable
Rolling
Upgrades instances in batches (percentage or count); health checks between batches
Production; mimics update domain sequential rollout for scale sets

# Set VMSS rolling upgrade policy (25% per batch, 20% max unhealthy)
az vmss update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVMSS \
  --set upgradePolicy.mode=Rolling \
  --set upgradePolicy.rollingUpgradePolicy.maxBatchInstancePercent=25 \
  --set upgradePolicy.rollingUpgradePolicy.maxUnhealthyInstancePercent=20 \
  --set upgradePolicy.rollingUpgradePolicy.maxUnhealthyUpgradedInstancePercent=20 \
  --set upgradePolicy.rollingUpgradePolicy.pauseTimeBetweenBatches=PT30S

# Start a rolling upgrade (e.g. after updating the VM image)
az vmss rolling-upgrade start \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVMSS

# Monitor rolling upgrade status
az vmss rolling-upgrade get-latest \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVMSS \
  --query "{status:runningStatus.code, progress:progress}" \
  --output json
Update Domains and Scheduled Events

Azure provides a metadata service endpoint inside VMs called Scheduled Events (IMDS). VMs can poll this endpoint to discover upcoming maintenance events before they occur, giving the application a chance to gracefully drain connections, checkpoint state, or perform a controlled shutdown.

# Poll the Scheduled Events metadata endpoint from inside a VM
# (Run this inside the VM, not from your workstation)
curl -H "Metadata:true" \
  "http://169.254.169.254/metadata/scheduledevents?api-version=2020-07-01"

# Example response (JSON):
# {
#   "DocumentIncarnation": 1,
#   "Events": [
#     {
#       "EventId": "abc123",
#       "EventType": "Reboot",
#       "ResourceType": "VirtualMachine",
#       "Resources": ["myVM-1"],
#       "EventStatus": "Scheduled",
#       "NotBefore": "2026-07-01T12:00:00Z"
#     }
#   ]
# }

Acknowledging a Scheduled Event signals to Azure that the VM is ready for maintenance. Azure may start the maintenance up to 5 minutes early once acknowledged. Use this to shorten the uncertainty window after you have finished draining connections.
IaC: Specifying Update Domains

resource "azurerm_availability_set" "example" {
  name                         = "myAS"
  resource_group_name          = azurerm_resource_group.example.name
  location                     = azurerm_resource_group.example.location
  platform_fault_domain_count  = 3
  platform_update_domain_count = 5   # Up to 20 for finer-grained rolling maintenance
  managed                      = true
}
Common Questions About Update Domains
Can I prevent Azure from rebooting my VMs during maintenance?

No, planned maintenance is mandatory for security and platform health. You can use Scheduled Events to prepare (drain, checkpoint), az vm redeploy to move the VM to a fresh host before the window, or Dedicated Hosts to control the maintenance schedule.
What is the minimum downtime per VM during a reboot maintenance?
Typically 10 to 15 minutes for a host reboot and VM restart sequence. Memory-preserving updates take under 30 seconds and do not trigger UD logic.
Are update domain rotations guaranteed to complete within a time bound?
No. The overall maintenance cycle can take days because Azure waits for each UD to pass health checks before proceeding to the next.
Can I change the number of update domains after creating an Availability Set?
No. Update domain count is immutable after AS creation. To change it, create a new AS with the desired count and migrate VMs.
Does increasing update domain count always improve availability?

More UDs mean fewer VMs are offline per maintenance cycle, but the overall cycle takes longer. 5 UDs is the right balance for most workloads; increase to 10-20 only if you need very fine-grained control.
Update Domain Assignment Algorithm

When VMs are added to an Availability Set, Azure assigns update domains in a round-robin pattern: the first VM gets UD0, the second UD1, the third UD2, and so on up to N-1, then wraps back to UD0. This deterministic assignment means you can predict which VMs will be in the same UD based on the order they were created.

The practical implication for multi-tier applications: if you deploy web VMs and app VMs in the same Availability Set (not recommended, but possible), a maintenance cycle targeting UD0 will reboot one web VM and one app VM simultaneously. This is why each tier should have its own Availability Set — so UD cycles in the web tier are independent of UD cycles in the app tier.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Simulate UD assignment for N VMs in an AS with M update domains
# This shows which VMs land in which UD (platform behavior)
N_VMS=9
M_UDS=5

echo "VM -> Update Domain Assignment (round-robin):"
for i in $(seq 1 $N_VMS); do
  UD=$(( (i - 1) % M_UDS ))
  echo "  VM-$i -> UD$UD"
done

# Verify actual assignments after deployment
az vm list --resource-group myRG --show-details \
  --query "[?availabilitySet!=null].{name:name, ud:instanceView.platformUpdateDomain}" \
  --output table
Planned vs Unplanned Maintenance: Which Gate Controls

Azure uses update domains for planned maintenance only. Unplanned events (hardware failures) are controlled by fault domains. Understanding which event type triggers which mechanism prevents confusion when diagnosing unexpected VM reboots.
Event Type
Domain Used
Trigger
Impact
Planned maintenance (reboot-required)
Update Domains
Host firmware/microcode update
Scheduled, 35-day notice, sequential UD rotation
Planned maintenance (memory-preserving)
N/A — no UD
Hypervisor live update
Under 30 seconds, no reboot, no UD logic needed
Unplanned hardware failure
Fault Domains
PDU, ToR switch, CPU, memory failure
Immediate, unscheduled, affects one fault domain
Host OS crash / panic
N/A — neither
Platform-level bug
Immediate restart of VM on same host or new host
Capacity pressure rebalancing
Neither
Platform initiated
Live migration (transparent); VM does not reboot
Dedicated Hosts: Taking Control of Maintenance Scheduling

If the default behavior (Azure schedules maintenance within a 35-day window) does not meet your operational requirements, Azure Dedicated Hosts let you control the maintenance schedule. When your VMs run on a Dedicated Host, you choose when the host maintenance occurs — you initiate it during a planned downtime window that suits your release calendar.

# Start maintenance on a Dedicated Host (customer-initiated)
az vm host redeploy \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --host-group myHostGroup \
  --name myDedicatedHost

# Check if maintenance is available to initiate
az vm host show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --host-group myHostGroup \
  --name myDedicatedHost \
  --query "{maintenance:platformUpdateDomain, autoReplaceOnFailure:autoReplaceOnFailure}"

# List all VMs on a dedicated host to know what will be rebooted
az vm list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[?host.id=='/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/hostGroups/myHostGroup/hosts/myDedicatedHost'].name" \
  --output tsv

With Dedicated Hosts, you accept responsibility for initiating maintenance. If you never trigger maintenance, Microsoft can still perform it as a last resort if the host develops a critical security vulnerability, but with maximum advance notice.
Calculating Capacity During Update Domain Maintenance
During a UD rotation, you lose 1/N of your VM capacity, where N is the number of update domains. You need to ensure your remaining N-1 of VMs can handle your peak load. The planning formula:
Determine peak requests per second (RPS) or CPU utilization at peak.
Determine your VM count in the AS (V).
Determine your UD count (N, default 5).
Calculate capacity during maintenance: VMs serving = V * (N-1)/N. Example: 10 VMs, 5 UDs = 10 * 4/5 = 8 VMs serving during maintenance.
Verify that 8 VMs can handle peak load with headroom (typically target 70-80% CPU to allow autoscaling to fill the gap).

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Capacity check: can the AS handle peak load with one UD offline?
RG="myRG"
AS="myAS"

# Get UD count and VM count
UD_COUNT=$(az vm availability-set show -g $RG -n $AS --query platformUpdateDomainCount -o tsv)
VM_COUNT=$(az vm list -g $RG --query "length([?availabilitySet.id!=null])" -o tsv)

VMS_SERVING=$(echo "scale=1; $VM_COUNT * ($UD_COUNT - 1) / $UD_COUNT" | bc)
echo "Availability Set: $AS"
echo "Total VMs: $VM_COUNT, Update Domains: $UD_COUNT"
echo "VMs serving during UD maintenance: $VMS_SERVING / $VM_COUNT"
echo ""
echo "Ensure these $VMS_SERVING VMs can handle peak traffic with <80% CPU."
echo "If not, add VMs to the AS or increase autoscaling buffer before maintenance window."

# Check current average CPU across all VMs in the AS (via Azure Monitor)
# (Requires az monitor metrics list per VM -- simplified here)
az vm list -g $RG --query "[?availabilitySet.id!=null].name" -o tsv | while read VM; do
  AVG_CPU=$(az monitor metrics list \
    --resource /subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)/resourceGroups/$RG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/$VM \
    --metric "Percentage CPU" \
    --interval PT5M \
    --aggregation Average \
    --query "value[0].timeseries[0].data[-1].average" \
    -o tsv 2>/dev/null)
  echo "  $VM: CPU avg = $AVG_CPU%"
done
Update Domains and Application Deployment Pipelines
Azure maintenance UD logic is analogous to rolling deployments in CI/CD. Understanding the parallel helps you design deployment pipelines that work safely alongside Azure's maintenance model:
Concept
Azure Maintenance (Update Domains)
Application Deployment (Rolling Deploy)
Unit of change
One update domain (1/N of VMs)
One deployment batch (percentage of instances)
Who triggers
Azure (scheduled)
Your CI/CD pipeline
Health gate
Platform checks VM heartbeat + LB probe
Your pipeline checks HTTP health endpoint, error rate
Rollback mechanism
Azure aborts if UD fails health check
Pipeline rolls back to previous version on health check failure
Overlap with Azure UD maintenance
You cannot control timing
Schedule deployments outside Azure maintenance windows if possible

If an Azure-initiated UD maintenance cycle starts at the same time as a CI/CD deployment, two sets of VMs could be in an unhealthy or transitioning state simultaneously. While unlikely to cause complete outage (FDs and UDs ensure some VMs always remain), it can complicate diagnosis. Best practice: use a deployment slot or blue/green deployment behind a traffic manager to decouple Azure maintenance from application rollouts.
Summary

Update domains are Azure's mechanism for ensuring that planned maintenance events (host reboots for firmware, hypervisor, or microcode updates) never take down all VMs in an Availability Set simultaneously. Azure reboots one update domain at a time, waits for health recovery, then moves to the next.
Each Availability Set has 5 update domains by default (up to 20); VMs are assigned to UDs round-robin as they are added.
Azure never reboots two update domains from the same AS simultaneously, ensuring N-1/N of VMs remain available during any planned maintenance cycle.
Planned maintenance events requiring reboots appear in Service Health at least 35 days in advance, giving time to prepare.
Use az vm redeploy to move a VM to a fresh host proactively and avoid the upcoming maintenance window.
Use the IMDS Scheduled Events endpoint inside VMs to detect imminent maintenance and gracefully drain connections before reboot.
VMSS rolling upgrade policy mirrors update domain behavior for scale set instances, letting you control upgrade batching and health gate thresholds.
